motivation_reason: want to keep mind off bidi
feel less like I need the bidi

session_notes.note: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
Mornings are the worst. I wake up, feel like I need something, and it's hard to resist. I know I shouldn't, but... I've tried before, and it didn't work.
I don't know, it's just a feeling like I need something in my mouth, and if I don't have it, I feel... empty, I suppose. Like I'm waiting for it.
I guess it's in my chest, feels a bit tight. But it's not so bad when I go for a walk or do some work. It's only when I'm sitting around, like on slow farm days.
I suppose it would help. I like taking care of the animals, anyway. But some days, I just feel like I'm waiting for something to do, and the bidi just becomes a habit, like a thing I always do.
I could check on the chickens more often, make sure the coop is clean. I like collecting the eggs, anyway. Maybe that would keep my mind off the bidi.
I might feel more awake, I guess. It's harder to think about smoking after I've been outside and got some work done. Maybe I won't even think about smoking.
Yeah, I think so. It's something to look forward to, and it might help me feel less like I need the bidi.
Boredom days are bad. But maybe if I have some small task to do, like fixing a tool or something, I won't think about smoking as much.
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.

Honestly, my wife has been telling me to stop for a while now. She's the one who keeps track of our expenses and says our money situation is getting tighter and tighter. She probably thinks my smoking is costing us money.

We could use that money for something more important, like paying the rent on time or maybe even starting a new business. But what if I don't succeed? I don't want to promise my wife something and then fail. That would just make things worse.

She's always saying I should quit, so maybe if we both work on it together, it'd be easier. But she gets stressed out too, and then I end up smoking when I see her getting frustrated. It's a vicious cycle.

No, we just yell at each other or go to sleep early. We're so tired all the time, I don't know what else to do. And when I try to talk to her about it, she just tells me to stop smoking, like that's the only problem.

I don't know... I've tried taking a few deep breaths when I'm working on the construction site, but it's hard to do when my coworkers are all smoking and asking me to join in. And it's not like I can just go somewhere quiet during work hours.
I guess it would be nice to have someone believe in me enough to think I can quit. My doctor said I had to come here, so that means he thinks I can do it too, right?

Honestly? I'm not sure. I'm still smoking, but...I don't know, it feels different now. Talking about it here has made me realize I don't want to be a slave to my coworkers and my stress. Maybe I can try to cut back or something.

I could try to cut back on the number of bidis I smoke each day. Maybe start with reducing it by one packet and see how it goes. But then what? And what if I'm at the construction site and everyone's smoking around me?
I think it would be when they offer me a bidi and say "have one, yaar". That's when I get pulled back in. It's like I'm stuck in a cycle. Maybe if I could just say no one time, it would be okay.
I don't know... They might think I'm weird or something. But what if they just respect my decision and leave me alone? Maybe it's worth trying, even if it feels silly.
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.

My daughter brought me here, actually. She's been saying I need to quit, because of my breathing problems. And it's true, in the cold weather, I get short of breath. But it's hard to stop, I've been doing it for so long.

I suppose it's both, but mostly I feel like I'm doing it for her. She's looking out for me, you know? I want to make her happy. But at the same time, I'm not getting younger... and it does make my breathing harder.

I'd be lost, I think. I wake up in the middle of the night, can't sleep, and reach for a bidi. That's my routine. I don't know what I'd do without it. The loneliness is hard, and smoking is one thing I don't have to think about.
I've tried reading, but my eyes get blurry. I've tried listening to music, but it's hard to focus. I miss my wife, and it's quiet at home. That's why I reach for the bidi, I guess. It's a habit.
If I felt like I had a plan, I guess I could see a way out of this. I wouldn't be as scared. I'd feel like I'm not alone in this. Maybe then, I could really try to quit for good.

It would need to be something that covers all the times when I feel lonely, and the nighttime cravings. And it would need to be something that's not just for me, but for my daughters too, so they can see me making progress and be proud of me. Maybe they could even be a part of it, you know?

They could check in on me at night, and see if I'm okay. Maybe they could come over and watch the serial with me. They could be my support, my friends. It's been a long time since I've had anyone to talk to about this stuff.
Yeah... I think so. It feels like I'm not giving up, like I'm finally doing something. I can see a way out, and it's not just about quitting, it's about reconnecting with my daughters too.

I'm looking forward to going a whole month without smoking. That's a big deal for me. Maybe after that, I can start doing more things with my daughters, like going for walks or going out for coffee. That would be nice.
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.

It's just... I don't know how to calm down. I get frustrated with my students and then I'm like, "Let's just go for a cigarette." It's like my mind tells me, "Smoking will help you relax." It does, for a little while, but then I feel guilty for smoking in the first place. My wife's always on my case about it too.
Like what? Taking a deep breath, yeah, that's about it. I guess I could try going for a walk or something. But it's not the same. When I'm angry, I need something to do with my hands, you know?

I don't know, just having some paper and a pencil probably. But what if I'm on a break at school and I don't have time to go find some paper? And what's the point of drawing if I'm still going to smoke when I get home?
A notebook's not a bad idea, but I've tried carrying one around before and it's just a hassle. And my phone's not really my thing, I'm not into drawing on it.

I don't know... It feels like I'm just substituting one habit for another, you know? But maybe it's not a bad thing if it helps me stop smoking. I just don't want to go from smoking 10 a day to drawing 10 doodles a day.

When I was a teacher trainee, I used to listen to music before class, but then I got a new job and the bus was really crowded, so I started listening to podcasts instead. It was nice to have something new to look forward to. Maybe drawing could be like that for me.

motivation_reason: want to prove quitting is worth it

session_notes.note: Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.

Let me think... I guess when I was a kid, my uncles used to smoke, but they didn't smoke all the time. I don't really remember feeling different, but I do recall asking my mom why they did it. Maybe it's because I didn't really get it then, but now I just associate it with being a part of my life, you know?

My wife was the one who actually convinced me to come here, to be honest. She's always nagging me about it, but I guess she genuinely wants me to stop for our kids' sake. I don't know, it's hard to take it seriously when they're still young and it's not like I'm affecting them directly. I mean, they see me smoking, but they don't really get it, right?

I think maybe it's a bit of both, but...I don't know, she's not really pushing me on it that much. I mean, she's always saying something, but it's not like she's going to leave me or anything. I think she's just trying to help, you know? And, okay, fine, maybe she is a bit worried about the kids inhaling secondhand smoke, but I told her I'll quit for them, that's good enough, right?

Let me think...okay, I remember when I used to play cricket when I was younger. I was actually pretty good, and I had to condition myself to exercise and push myself to be better. But that was different, because I enjoyed it. I loved the rush of adrenaline and feeling like I could do something. With quitting smoking, it's just...I don't know, it doesn't feel the same. I don't see the point in it. Unless...unless it's for my wife, I guess.

I don't know...I guess if I could see some kind of benefit for myself, maybe something that would make a real difference. Not just some vague promise of being healthier or something, but something tangible. Like if I could find a way to quit that would make my life better in some way, like maybe more energy or being able to walk up stairs without getting winded...then maybe I could get on board with it.

Actually, I have a cousin's wedding coming up in a few months. It's going to be a big ceremony, and I'll have to walk down the aisle. That's like, a mile, right? If I can get through that without running out of breath or feeling like I'm going to keel over, that would be something. That would prove to me that quitting smoking is actually worth it.

Yeah, I guess I could start doing some light exercise, like walking around the block or something. But I don't know, it's hard to get motivated to do that when I'm used to just sitting around all day. Maybe if I could find a way to make it more enjoyable...like if I could walk with a friend or something. That way, it wouldn't feel so boring.

Cravings? I don't know...I guess I hadn't thought about it that much. But, um, if I'm being honest, I think a cigarette would be the last thing on my mind if I'm walking with my brother and talking about old times. The walk itself would probably be enough to keep my mind off smoking.

Um...I don't know, I guess I'd just...stop walking and take a few deep breaths, or something. Just calm down and remind myself why I'm doing this. I could also, I don't know, distract myself by telling my brother a funny story or something. Just find something to take my mind off it, I suppose.

motivation_reason: feeling like I'm not capable and competent

session_notes.note: I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

I think it's the weight gain. Every time I try to quit, I gain so much weight. I've tried nicotine gum, patches, even going cold turkey, but it's just the weight gain that gets to me. I'm already worried about getting older and I don't want to be bigger.

It's just hard for me to accept that my body is changing so much. I feel like I'm losing control, you know? And it's not just the weight itself, it's the way I feel about myself. I'm already not feeling great about my life and my job and stuff, and then I'm gaining all this weight... it makes me feel like I'm failing at everything.

It's just... I have a lot of responsibilities at work and at home, and I feel like I'm not doing any of it well. I'm always stressed out, and I feel like I'm barely holding it together. And then I look at myself and I just feel like I'm not taking care of myself, like I'm neglecting my own needs. It's hard for me to see myself as capable and competent, you know? I just feel like I'm drowning.

Well... I used to be really good at my job, before things started getting so stressful. I was actually pretty good at it, and I felt like I was making a difference. But that's a long time ago, before my boss started breathing down my neck all the time... *pauses, getting choked up* Sorry, it's just hard to think about.

I... I think it would take a change, you know? A change in my job, or my life, something that would make me feel like I'm moving forward, like I'm not just stuck in this rut. I don't know, maybe that sounds silly, but it's what I feel. And maybe, just maybe, quitting smoking would be a step in the right direction. But then I think about the weight gain and everything that comes with it... *sighs* I don't know if I'm ready for that yet.

I guess... I'd feel like I failed again. Worse than that, I'd feel like I'm not capable of doing anything right, that I'm just one big failure. I'd probably just go back to smoking, and then feel guilty about that too. *looks down, shaking head* It's just a never-ending cycle, and I don't know how to get out of it.

Well... there was a time when I went through a really tough breakup, and I was devastated. But I had to keep going, for my kids. I had to be strong for them, even when I felt like falling apart. And I did it. I got through it, and I came out okay on the other side. It's not the same as quitting smoking, I know, but it makes me think that maybe I am capable of pushing through hard things and coming out stronger. Maybe.

I think it's that I can be strong for myself, even when it feels like everything is falling apart. I mean, during that breakup, it felt like my whole world was ending, but I didn't let it consume me. I found a way to get through it, to take care of myself and my kids. And I think that's what I need to do with quitting smoking - take care of myself, one day at a time.

Well... taking care of myself means not letting smoking control me anymore. It means finding healthier ways to deal with stress and boredom, like going for walks or reading books. And I think I can start by doing just one of those things today, like going for a short walk during my lunch break. Or maybe just cutting back on how much I smoke, instead of quitting cold turkey. Just taking it one step at a time, you know?

That's a good idea. I used to love listening to music when I was stressed, and I haven't done that in ages. Maybe I can make an effort to listen to some calming music or nature sounds while I'm walking. It would make the experience feel more special and help me unwind. Like, maybe I could even find a playlist on Spotify that's specifically for walking or relaxation, and listen to that while I'm on my break. That sounds like a nice way to start the day.

motivation_reason: fear of losing a part of life
want to hang out with them without smoking
feel like I'm on the right track

triggers.trigger: thinking about where I can smoke next

session_notes.note: I don't think I could handle that. It's not just about quitting, it's about who I am. Smoking is a big part of my social life, and I don't want to be that guy who can't hang out with the others.

I don't know... I guess I just see myself as a smoker. It's hard to imagine being someone else. I feel like it's a part of my life that's always been there, and stopping it would be like... I don't know, losing a friend, I guess.

I guess if someone could help me figure out how to deal with my coworkers, you know, without being a total outcast. If I could still hang out with them without smoking, that would make it a lot easier.
Yeah, I guess that's something I could try. But it's hard when they all smoke so much. Maybe I could just start by not smoking at work, just for a few hours, and see how it goes.
Yeah, that might actually feel pretty good. It's weird, I'm actually starting to think it's possible to hang out with them without smoking.
It might actually make me feel pretty proud of myself, you know? Like, I'd be showing them that I can be strong and stick to something, even if it's hard.

That would be really cool. I think it would make me feel like I'm on the right track, and like I can actually do this. Maybe we could even go out for a meal or something, and I wouldn't have to be thinking about where I can smoke next.

Yeah, I could start looking at some fun things we could do together, like going to a cricket match or trying that new restaurant in town. That way, I'll have something to look forward to when I get to a certain point in my quit.

past_strategies.strategy: walking out of the office for a few minutes

session_notes.note: I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.

It was the constant pressure to meet deadlines, I guess. Every meeting felt like an interrogation, and if I didn't come up with a solution on the spot, I'd get yelled at. It was just really overwhelming.
I don't know... I feel like I'm using it as a crutch, but I'm also not sure how to deal with stress without it. At least when I was smoking, I felt like I could function, you know?

I guess it would be being able to take a step back, think things through, and not feel like I'm on edge all the time. Like, being able to say 'you know what, I'm not going to sweat this meeting, I'll just do what I can and it'll be fine.'

I guess... I did quit for 3 months last year. And even though I relapsed, it felt like a small victory after having made it for that long. Maybe that's what I need to focus on, not the relapse, but the progress I made.
I think I learned that I can do it for a long time, I just need a better strategy for dealing with stress. And maybe... just maybe, relapsing isn't a complete failure. It's just a setback.

Yeah... taking those deep breaths before meetings was a good start. Maybe I could also try walking out of the office for a few minutes during my lunch break, instead of just staring at my computer screen. Get some fresh air, clear my head.
I smoke because it calms me down when I feel anxious. I know it makes things worse but I can't stop.
My grandmother used to make me tea when I was stressed, it was like a warm hug in a cup. I haven't tried any new teas since I started smoking, but maybe I could try that again.

I think I could... um... buy some tea at the store. But then I'd have to remember to make it, and it's just so easy to grab a cigarette instead. It's hard when I'm working on a tight deadline and my mind is racing.
I don't know... maybe 5-10 minutes, I guess. But what if I forget to make the tea, or I'm really overwhelmed and there's no time? I don't want to feel like I'm failing at this, too.

My partner always says I'm too hard on myself, maybe I could take that as a reminder to be kind to myself during those 5 minutes, too. I'll try to use it to breathe, or meditate, or just sit still for a bit.

My therapist told me to write down kind things to myself during the day, like affirmations. Maybe I could write them on my sticky note with the tea reminder. That way, I'll see them whenever I'm stressed and remember to be kind.

I guess I could keep a small journal just for my affirmations, and write in it every time I take a tea break. That way, I can reflect on how I'm feeling and rewrite my affirmations if I need to. It's like, a little tea-and-affirmations ritual.
Daily sounds like too much, but maybe every time I take a tea break, like I said before. That way, it's something I'm already doing, and I can just add the journaling to it.
I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.
I guess I feel like it's just a part of who I am when I'm out with friends. Everyone smokes when we're out, so it's like it's expected, you know?
I mean, I've tried that before, and it's not that bad. But it feels like I'm missing out on something, you know? My friends are all smoking and laughing, and I'm just standing there drinking a soda.
It's really important, yeah. I don't want to be the guy who's always sitting on the outside looking in. I want to be able to hang out with my friends and feel like I'm one of them.
I don't know, maybe I'll just tell him that I need more time or that I'm working on it. I don't want to admit defeat in front of him, so I'll probably just say I'm good and that I'll quit soon.

I guess I could be like, "Hey, I'm really trying to quit, but it's tough in situations like parties. Can you help me out and talk me through it when I'm feeling tempted?" That way I'm being honest about my struggle without saying I'm failing.

I don't know, that would be pretty rough, I guess. But if he said no, it would mean I'd have to think of someone else to turn to, right? Maybe I could talk to my sister, she's always been really supportive of me trying to quit.

My sister's a health nut, always on my case about eating healthy and exercising. She's always been really supportive when I've tried to quit smoking before, so I think she'd be good to talk to about it again. Plus, she's not as close to the party scene as Alex is, so maybe she wouldn't be able to relate as much to that part of it.

I think talking to her might help me see things from a different perspective. She's always been really good at finding solutions to problems, and I'm sure she'd help me think of some new strategies for dealing with the party scene. Plus, just talking to her about it might help me feel more accountable, you know? Like, if I'm telling her about my struggles, then I've got to actually do something about it.

I'm actually going to one of those big birthday bashes for Alex's friend in a few weeks, and I know it's going to be a total party scene. I was just thinking about how I'm going to handle it, and talking to my sister about it might actually be really helpful. She's always been good at finding ways for me to cope with tough situations, so I'm hoping she can help me come up with a plan for that party.
I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.

Work. It's just so stressful. And during meetings, I usually need to be sharp, and that's when I crave a cigarette the most. It's like my mind tells me it'll help me focus or calm down, but I know it's not true, it's just a habit.
I guess it could work, but what if it doesn't? What if I'm still really craving a cigarette? I don't want to feel like I'm failing again.

Yeah, I had to change jobs a few years ago, it was really tough. But I went after it and I got the job I wanted. It was hard, but it was worth it. I guess what I learned is that I'm stronger than I think I am. Maybe I can be stronger than my cravings too.
I don't know, something like... "you got this, Kavitha." Like a little phrase that repeats in my head. But, what if it doesn't work? What if I need to remind myself of something else?

There was this one time when I had to give a presentation at work, and I was so nervous. But I practiced really hard and I did it well. The whole room was silent after I finished, and then people started clapping, and I felt this huge sense of relief and pride. I think what I was thinking at that moment was... "I did it, I actually did it." And feeling proud of myself like that would be really helpful now, I think.

Yes, I think I could imagine that moment, standing there with everyone clapping, and feeling so proud of myself. But, what if my mind starts to wander and I get distracted? Like, I might start thinking about something else and I'll forget about that feeling.

I guess I could just take a few deep breaths, say "clap" to myself, and remind myself why I'm quitting. Like, I'm doing it for my daughter, and I'm strong enough to handle this. I don't need a cigarette to get through it.

That's a good point, I do get cravings pretty early in the morning. I think I can try setting a timer to remind me to take a few deep breaths and say "clap" to myself, like, right after I wake up. And maybe even make a plan for the day, like, what I'll do to avoid stressful situations or how I'll handle them if they come up. That way, I'll feel more prepared and in control.

motivation_reason: trying to avoid falling back into the habit

session_notes.note: I chew gutkha, not cigarettes. About 8 to 10 pouches a day for the last 7 years. My mouth has developed some kind of white patch and the doctor is worried.

It's scary to think about... but I have to do something. I don't want to die from this, you know? But my gutkha pouches are always with me, even in the rickshaw. What am I supposed to do without them? I need something to do with my hands when I'm waiting for passengers.

Hmm, I don't know... I've tried fidgeting with rubber bands before, but they get lost. I've also seen drivers play with their phones, but that's not good, right? What if I got a stress ball or something? But it's not the same, I mean, I'd miss the taste of gutkha...

Gum? I don't know... I'm not really a gum person. But sunflower seeds, yeah, that's a good idea. I eat those already, actually. I like the taste and it's healthy, right? But I'm not sure it's the same as sucking on gutkha.

Water? Yeah, that's a good idea. When I'm stuck in traffic or waiting for passengers, I can just drink some water and eat sunflower seeds. It's simple, I guess. I just need to get used to doing it instead of reaching for gutkha.

It's the bored moments, I think. When I'm just sitting at a red light or during a long trip, it's hard to just sit there with nothing to do. And if I'm bored, I'm more likely to grab my gutkha. But... I also think about my mom. She's always getting cancer scares because of her tobacco, and I don't want to end up like that. Maybe that'll give me the strength to keep going.

Music, maybe? I listen to music all the time, but sometimes I just get in the habit of chewing gutkha while I'm listening. If I'm really bored, maybe I can just put on a song I like and sing along instead. Or think about a funny story or something. It's not the same as gutkha, but it's something, right?

I don't know... Maybe it's too easy to fall back into the habit, you know? I've been doing this for 7 years. But I'm trying, I really am. What if I also keep sunflower seeds in the rickshaw, just in case? That way, I've got something to do with my hands and something healthy to eat if I'm feeling really stuck.

If I could just get through the next week or two without buying any gutkha, I think I'd feel pretty good about it. And if I could show my doctor that the white patch is going away, that'd be amazing. I just need to feel like I'm making progress, you know? Little by little, I can do this.

The biggest challenge is probably the first few days. I'm used to having gutkha with me all the time, and it's hard to just stop. But if I can get through those first few days, I think it'll get easier. I'm just going to take it one day at a time, one trip at a time. And if I'm feeling really desperate, I'll remind myself about my mom and why I'm doing this.

Knowing that I've got sunflower seeds and water waiting for me in the rickshaw, that would help a lot. And maybe if I could find a few songs to listen to that really get me pumped up or relaxed when I'm feeling anxious, that would help too. If I've got those things, I think I'll be okay. I just need to get used to the idea of being without gutkha for longer periods of time.

motivation_reason: trying to quit for the team
fear of being seen as unwanted

triggers.trigger: fear of being seen as unwanted

session_notes.note: I started smoking 2 years ago in college. My friends all do it. I'm on the cricket team and my stamina is getting really bad now.
I guess I could try not smoking for one day, see if it makes a difference. But what about when my friends ask me to hang out and smoke? That's when it's hardest, I think.

Maybe I could just leave them for a bit, go get some water or something, and come back. Or... or they might understand if I told them I've been trying to quit for the team. Maybe that would make them see it in a different way.

That sounds kind of easy, yeah. I could just say that and not feel like I'm missing out or letting them down. But what if they all start teasing me or think I'm quitting because they're trying to get rid of me?
It would probably be a bit of both. Their words would sting, but it's also like, what if they're right? What if I am quitting because they're getting rid of me? It's hard to shake that feeling off.

I guess it would be like... I'd be doing it for myself and the team, not for them. It's about being the best player I can be, not about what they think of me. But how do I actually make that happen? It's not that easy, is it?

I could write it down or put it on my phone's lock screen, so every time I'm about to light up or hang out with them, I see it and remember why I'm doing this. That way, it's easy to focus on my own reasons, not theirs.

I could leave my phone on the desk at home or in my bag, so when I'm out with my friends and I'm feeling tempted, I have to think about it more, instead of just reaching for my phone. It would make me take a step back and remember why I'm quitting.

Honestly, I'm not sure. It'll be hard, especially if they're all smoking and I'm feeling left out. But I really want to be able to play cricket like I used to, and I feel like this is the only way to make it happen. I'll try my best, at least.

I guess I could just say no to a cigarette if they offer it to me. That's a small step, but it's something, right? And then I could try to focus on the things we're doing together, like playing a game or watching a match, instead of getting caught up in the smoking.
My son brought me here. I smoke 4-5 bidis a day and eat paan. I've been doing this for 30 years. I don't think I can stop at this age.
My age, the doctor told me I have some COPD. I see people much younger than me smoking and they seem fine.
Shortness of breath, sometimes it comes back after I've rested. My doctor says it's mild now, but it will get worse. I'm 58, my life is already half done.
My grandchildren, I want to see them grow up. See their weddings, become grandparents myself. But now I'm worried, I won't be able to keep up with them, it's all so... uncertain.

They would see how hard I worked for them, how I always put them first. My husband passed away before their father, he was the one to look up to. Now I'm the only one they have left, and I want to be strong for them.
If I quit, I'll be able to run around with them, play with them, not have to catch my breath all the time. They'll see that even at my age, I can still be healthy and happy.
What if everyone is smoking around me? I don't know, it's hard to resist when everyone is doing it.
They would be so proud, they would tell their parents, "Look, Grandma is strong, she doesn't smoke." They would look up to me even more.

My granddaughter, she's 10 years old, she always wants to sit by me and talk. She says I'm the strongest woman she knows. I can see her face when I'm feeling down, it gives me the will to not give up.

I can carry a picture of her in my purse, or I can look at old photos of her when I'm feeling weak. That would remind me of why I'm doing this. Or maybe I can just close my eyes and remember her saying I'm strong.

I'll just remember what she said, or I'll take a few deep breaths, or I'll think about all the things I want to do with her, like taking her to the park or cooking her favorite meal. These things will remind me of why I'm quitting.

motivation_reason: being more aware of smoking and drinking

triggers.trigger: social situations with people who drink and smoke

past_strategies.strategy: quit smoking for a single day

session_notes.note: I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.

I guess it's just a habit now. When I'm drinking, I feel like I need something else to do with my hands, and smoking just comes naturally. It starts with one or two, but then it's like I'm on autopilot after that.
Try it just once? That sounds too easy to fail. What if I just end up having a drink and then light a cigarette anyway? That's exactly what happened last time I tried something like this.
I guess I feel like I'm letting myself down. And my wife. I don't want to have to admit to her that I couldn't even manage to quit smoking for a single day on a weekend when I'm drinking.

Yeah... maybe. I mean, it's not like I've been keeping it a secret from her or anything. But, I don't know, it's hard to explain. Maybe if I just try something small and it doesn't work out, and I talk to her about it, it might help? But what if she's disappointed in me, or... I don't know.

Yeah... there was that one time when I was really stressed out at work and I came home late, and I just snapped at her. But she just listened to me and calmed me down, and we talked it through. She's always been like that, trying to help me see things from a different perspective. Maybe she can do the same with this.

If I talk to her beforehand, she might be more understanding if I slip up, or she could even just remind me to be careful. And who knows, maybe just having that conversation will make me more aware of my smoking and drinking, and I'll be able to catch myself before I have too many.

I think I would say something like, "Hey, I know I've been trying to quit smoking, and I'm really struggling with it this weekend because I know I'll be drinking. Can we just talk about it and figure out a plan together, so I can stay on track?"

Maybe she could come with us, so I wouldn't have to drink as much, or we could just plan to meet up with other friends for a bit, so I'm not hanging out with the guys who always drink and smoke. Or she could just be prepared to leave early if I'm getting too carried away with the drinks.
I've been smoking for about 2 years. My husband and I are planning to have a baby soon. I know I need to stop.
He's been really supportive, actually. He's the one who's been pushing me to quit. He doesn't smoke, so he's always been against it.

It means a lot, because he's my rock. Just knowing that he's there for me, even if I slip up, helps me stay on track. Plus, it's weird, but just thinking about having his support and being able to have a healthy baby together makes me feel more motivated.

Well, my biggest stressors are at school – like during breaks or when I'm dealing with a lot of work. I've been thinking maybe I could use that time to go for a walk or do some deep breathing exercises instead of reaching for a cigarette.

Honestly? It's just that my default reaction is always to reach for a cigarette when I'm stressed or anxious. It's like an automatic habit, you know? I'm not sure how often I'll actually remember to take deep breaths instead of grabbing a cigarette.
One specific situation that comes to mind is when I'm grading papers during my free period – it's just a really stressful, monotonous task, and I often find myself smoking during that time.

I guess I could try to take a few deep breaths before I even start grading, just to calm down a bit. And then if I'm feeling really overwhelmed, I could get up and take a short walk to the school garden or something. That way, I'm not just sitting there, feeling stressed, and reaching for a cigarette.

That's a good question... I think if I'm able to stay smoke-free during my next free period, it would be a good feeling to know that I can overcome that particular trigger. Maybe I could even reward myself with something small, like going for a longer walk or getting a cup of coffee after school. Just something to give me a sense of accomplishment and motivation to keep going.

I'd like to treat myself to that reward every time I successfully stay smoke-free for a day, at least for the first week or so. If I can make it a consistent habit, it might help motivate me to keep going.

motivation_reason: taking care of myself and my health

session_notes.note: I work from home and since 2020 my smoking has more than doubled. I used to smoke 5 a day and now it's 18-20.

Procrastination's a big one, I guess. When I'm stuck on a task, I tend to take a break and grab a cigarette. Also, when I'm switching between tasks, I find myself smoking more as a way to get my brain to switch gears. And, to be honest, the isolation can be tough sometimes.

I'd say it's boredom, I guess. When I'm stuck, I start feeling like I'm not making progress, and that's frustrating. And then, before I know it, I'm lighting up. It's like my brain just wants something to do, and smoking becomes a default.

Well, I've tried breaking tasks into smaller chunks before, but it's hard to stick to it. And I've also tried setting timers, like the Pomodoro technique, but I always end up getting distracted. I did have a bit of success with it the other day, though - I set a timer for 25 minutes and just focused on my code, and didn't smoke during that time. But it was just one time...

Actually, I think it was because I had a deadline looming at the end of the day, so I was really motivated to get something done. And, weirdly, not having my wife around, because she's always nagging me about smoking, might have made a difference too. I don't know, it's hard to say. But maybe if I set smaller deadlines for myself, like getting a certain task done by the end of the day, it could help?

Yeah, I think it would be really freeing, you know? Not having to rely on cigarettes when I'm feeling stuck or bored. It's like, if I could just focus on the task and make progress, I'd feel more in control, I guess. To create that feeling consistently...I don't know, maybe I need to make a schedule for my day, or track my progress somehow, like I used to when I was trying to quit. That way, I can see how far I've come and feel a sense of accomplishment.

Honestly, I've always struggled with morning routines. I'm not a morning person, and I often find excuses not to get up early. But I do have a gym membership, and I've been going there after work. Maybe I could try doing that in the morning instead? It would get me moving and give me a sense of accomplishment before I even start my workday.

Yeah, that's a good point. If there's a delay, I might get frustrated and want to give up. But maybe I can use that as an opportunity to focus on my breathing, or think about why I'm doing this in the first place. It's not just about getting to the gym, it's about taking care of myself and my health, and that's worth waiting a few minutes for.
I used to smoke one and a half bundles of bidis a day. That's about 25. Now I'm down to 2 or 3. My doctor says I should stop completely.
It's like I thought I'd never get here, but I did. Now the last few are harder to quit, especially the morning one. That's the only one I still smoke every day.
I don't know... it's scary to think about. If I skip it, will I still be able to focus on my work? I get anxious if my mind is not sharp.
I already write down things, but I guess it would help to make a habit of it first thing in the morning. But what about when I'm not at my desk? When I'm out and about...
That sounds like a good idea, but will I be able to stick to it? I've set reminders before and forgotten about them.
I think it's doable now. I have a plan, and I've reduced my smoking already. If I can get through one day without that morning bidi, it'll be a big step, I think.

I think it'll be when I get out of bed. That's when the craving hits. If I could find something else to do in those first few minutes, maybe I could avoid it. A cup of tea, but not with a bidi... just the tea.
That's a good idea. I'll do that. And then maybe I can try to do some deep breathing or something while I wait for the tea to be ready.
I'll try it. But just for a few days, and then we can see how it's going. If it's not working, we can try something else.
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. I calculate I spend about 4000 rupees a month on cigarettes. My family is struggling and I feel guilty about it.
It would be a huge relief, you know. School fees are due soon and my wife is stressing about it. We really can't afford to waste that kind of money on cigarettes.

We could really use it to pay off the school fees for my kids, and maybe even set aside some for their future expenses. My wife has been worrying about how we're going to manage, and this would really take a weight off her shoulders.

Honestly, the traffic stress and long delivery hours make it hard for me to manage without cigarettes. I find myself reaching for them during those times when I'm alone in the cab. It's like it's become a habit to have one in that situation.

Actually, I've been listening to a lot of history podcasts lately. They're really interesting and can keep me engaged. Maybe I could listen to those instead of reaching for cigarettes. Do you think it would really make a difference if I just replaced the smoking with listening to history podcasts?
I'll stop and assess why I slipped up, you know, what triggered it. Was it just a moment of stress or was something else going on? Maybe I can figure out what it was and adjust my plan for next time.

I think I could ask myself if there was something else I could have done instead of reaching for a cigarette, like taking a few deep breaths or calling my wife to talk. Maybe I can think of some other alternative actions I can take to manage the stress.

I think it'll make a big difference. When I feel the urge to smoke, I'll just glance at my phone and see the reminder. It'll be like a little nudge to pause and take a few deep breaths instead of reaching for a cigarette. It's a small thing, but it might be enough to break the habit.
I only smoke at parties or when I'm drinking with friends, maybe 3-4 times a week. It's not like I'm a real smoker. But I've been having asthma attacks more often.
I guess I could try keeping track of when these asthma attacks happen and see if there's a pattern, like is it always after I've smoked.
If I found out they're connected, I'd think about... skipping cigarettes when I'm out with friends, just to see if it makes a difference.
I don't know, maybe if my doctor would give me some advice on how to control the asthma attacks without smoking, and if my friends wouldn't think I'm weird for not smoking at parties.

My doctor mentioned something about prescription medication to help with quitting, and I think that could be helpful. Plus, if she said it was for my asthma, I guess that would be a good reason for me to quit.
I'm not sure... it sounds kind of scary to be taking medicine for my brain, you know? I don't want to be dependent on something else to stop smoking.

Dependency means being stuck on something I don't want to be on. I don't want to be taking medicine for the rest of my life just to quit smoking. It seems like it would be a hassle, and what if it had side effects? I'd rather find a way to stop smoking for good.

I guess if I could just stop smoking for a week or two without any trouble, that would be a good start. Like, if I could just get through a few parties without smoking, I'd feel more confident that I could do it long-term.
I think I could try to stay away from the smoking areas or leave early if it gets too tempting. And maybe if I could find some non-smoking friends to hang out with, that would be easier.
I'd probably just say something like, "No thanks, I'm trying to quit smoking" or "I'm not feeling like smoking right now." Easy, I've heard people say that before.

That would be tougher... I think I'd want to explain to them why I'm trying to quit, and maybe ask for their support instead. Like, "Hey, I'm trying to quit smoking for my asthma, would you please not offer me cigarettes when we hang out?"
I think they would be understanding and supportive, they know about my asthma problems. They might even be proud of me for trying to quit.
